card.title: How Gerrymandering Works
card.teaser: How can 60% of voters lose a 'fair' election? By drawing the map first.
card.coverImagePrompt: A U.S. state map being redrawn with red and blue ink, lines twisting into absurd salamander-like shapes, close-up of an ink pen mid-stroke, cinematic and ominous

lessons.title: The salamander that started it
lessons.body: In 1812, Massachusetts Governor Elbridge Gerry signed off on an election district so twisted it resembled a salamander. A Boston newspaper mashed his name with the creature — 'Gerry-mander' — and the term stuck for 200 years. The same trick Gerry used in 1812 is still reshaping democracies today, just with better software.

visual.imagePrompt: A 19th-century political cartoon of the original 'Gerry-mander' district, drawn as a winged salamander with claws, sepia tones

lessons.title: The two dirty tricks
lessons.body: Gerrymandering has two main techniques: packing and cracking. Packing crams your opponents into a few districts so they win those by huge margins but lose everywhere else. Cracking splits them across many districts so they're always a minority. Either way, their votes get 'wasted.' Used together, a party that loses the popular vote can still win most seats.

keyTerms.definition: Concentrating opposing voters into as few districts as possible
Splitting opposing voters across many districts so they can't win any
Any vote cast for a losing candidate, or beyond what a winner needed

visual.imagePrompt: A grid of 50 red and blue dots sorted into three possible district maps showing different outcomes, minimalist infographic

lessons.title: The math of manipulation
lessons.body: Picture a state with 50 voters: 30 blue, 20 red, divided into 5 districts of 10. Fair math says blue should win about 3 seats. But if red packs 10 blue voters into District 1 and cracks the remaining 20 across Districts 2–5 (5 blue vs 5 red each), red can win 4 of 5 seats with just 40% of the vote. Same voters. Different lines. Opposite result.

visual.imagePrompt: A side-by-side comparison of two district maps with identical voters producing opposite election outcomes, clean vector style

lessons.title: The efficiency gap
lessons.body: In 2015, political scientists Nicholas Stephanopoulos and Eric McGhee proposed a measurable test called the 'efficiency gap' — the difference in wasted votes between parties, divided by total votes. North Carolina's 2016 congressional map produced an efficiency gap of around 20%, meaning Republicans won 10 of 13 seats with about 53% of the statewide vote. Courts later ruled the map an unconstitutional partisan gerrymander.
lessons.funFact: Modern gerrymandering uses block-level demographic data and optimization algorithms — mapmakers can now tune a district to the nearest city block.

visual.imagePrompt: A detailed North Carolina congressional district map with thin tentacle-like boundaries, annotated with vote percentages

lessons.title: The fix is possible
lessons.body: Several countries and U.S. states have handed district-drawing to independent commissions instead of politicians — Canada, Australia, and states like California and Michigan. The results are measurably fairer maps and more competitive elections. Gerrymandering isn't a law of nature. It's a choice about who gets to hold the pen.

quiz.question: What are the two main techniques of partisan gerrymandering?
quiz.explanation: Packing concentrates opposing voters; cracking disperses them. Both waste opposition votes.

quiz.question: Where does the word 'gerrymander' come from?
quiz.explanation: In 1812 a Boston newspaper named a twisted district after Governor Gerry's signature and its salamander shape.

quiz.question: What is one proven way to reduce gerrymandering?
options: Letting the legislature draw maps faster
quiz.explanation: Independent commissions in places like California, Michigan, and Canada tend to produce measurably fairer maps.

sources.title: Partisan Gerrymandering and the Efficiency Gap
